flags.concern: Removing `ClipAction`'s `onPointerDown` `stopPropagation` is not supported by the current event flow and risks reintroducing competing timeline-library drags.
flags.evidence: `ClipAction` currently blocks bubbled `pointerdown` at `src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/ClipAction.tsx:45`. In `useClipDrag`, the wrapper listener runs in capture phase (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts:425-426`), but pointer capture is only taken after the move threshold inside `handlePointerMove` (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts:318-323`). That means a bubbled `pointerdown` can still reach the timeline library before this hook has actually claimed the drag, so the plan's rationale that pointer capture already handles it is weaker than the repository code supports.

flags.concern: Step 7 is under-scoped and internally inconsistent: the proposed coordinator still exposes the raw ref, so it would not actually eliminate direct `ActionDragState` mutation in this repository.
flags.evidence: The plan's proposed `ActionDragCoordinator` includes `ref: MutableRefObject<Record<string, ActionDragState>>`, but the success criteria say there should be no direct `ref.current[id] = ...` outside the coordinator. In the repo, that raw ref is part of the public plumbing already: `useTimelineData` exports `actionDragStateRef` (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineData.ts:84`), `useTimelineState` exposes and forwards it (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineState.ts:25`, `src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineState.ts:112`, `src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineState.ts:149`), and `TimelineEditor` passes it into `useClipDrag` (`src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx:26`, `src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx:62`). Updating only `useLibraryCallbacks.ts` and `useClipDrag.ts` will not complete the migration.

flags.concern: Over-engineering: Step 1 adds a new drop-indicator hook for external DnD but leaves the existing duplicate indicator implementation in `useClipDrag`, so the plan adds abstraction without removing the main duplication point.
flags.evidence: `useTimelineEditing` has one indicator implementation (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineEditing.ts:155-204`), while `useClipDrag` has another with its own DOM creation and inline z-index styles (`src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useClipDrag.ts:93-118`). `TimelineEditor` explicitly describes internal clip drag and external HTML5 drag-drop as using the same indicator system (`src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx:47-49`). As written, the plan extracts only the external side, so two indicator codepaths would remain.
